one_line_takeaway: A broad cross-disciplinary survey arguing forgetting in deep learning is a double-edged sword — harmful for knowledge retention but beneficial for privacy (machine unlearning), overfitting, and debiasing — rather than a security paper.
key_finding: The paper's central thesis (not a metric — it reports no experimental numbers) is that forgetting is a double-edged sword: harmful in most knowledge-retention/adaptation settings (where distribution or task shift erodes previously learned knowledge), yet deliberately beneficial in specific scenarios — namely privacy protection via machine unlearning (aligning with GDPR / the right to be forgotten and defending against membership-inference and backdoor attacks), mitigating overfitting/memorization, and debiasing. It also notes machine unlearning itself induces a secondary catastrophic forgetting of the remaining (non-targeted) data, and that approximate unlearning trades off privacy guarantees against utility relative to differential privacy (DP being sufficient but not necessary for ε-approximate unlearning).
core_contribution: This is a survey paper (Wang, Yang, Shen, Huang), not an empirical or methods paper. Its own contribution, as stated in its three-fold contribution list (paper's Section 1, "our contributions can be summarized into three fold") and conclusion, is: (1) a more systematic categorization of continual-learning (CL) problem settings and methods than prior CL surveys; (2) extending the study of forgetting beyond CL to seven additional domains — foundation models, domain adaptation, test-time adaptation, meta-learning, generative models, reinforcement learning, and federated learning; and (3) advancing a "double-edged sword" thesis that forgetting is not always harmful but can be deliberately beneficial (e.g., mitigating overfitting, debiasing/removing irrelevant information, and privacy-preserving machine unlearning). The concrete techniques it discusses (SISA, DeltaGrad, ARCANE, Certified Removal, VBU, L-CODEC, UNLEARN, Forsaken, PUMA, etc.) are cited prior work being surveyed, NOT methods introduced by this paper.
limitations: As a survey it introduces no new method, runs no experiments, and provides no empirical comparison, so claims about which techniques work best rest entirely on cited sources. Coverage is broad but shallow per domain, and (as of the v3 Nov-2024 arXiv version) it predates much recent LLM-unlearning work. For an AI-security knowledge graph its usefulness is narrow: only Section 9.3 (machine unlearning, DP connection, MIA/backdoor defense) is security-relevant, and even that summarizes external work rather than contributing new security results.

quantitative_results: No experimental/quantitative results are reported — this is a survey with no experiments of its own. Its three tables are qualitative: Table 1 compares harmful-forgetting problem settings by goal and source of forgetting; Table 2 compares beneficial-forgetting settings; Table 3 is a content outline/taxonomy of continual-learning settings. No accuracy, forgetting-rate, or benchmark numbers are produced by the authors.
